quotes: I have been a teacher for 29 years in English
I also worked for a company where I was a content director, a content developer, I'm a subject matter expert
I noticed that the assessments didn't, they didn't align with what we were actually teaching, and so it was often that we had to go back and correct those assessments
role_fit_and_relevant_experience.rationale: Leslie has 29 years of English teaching experience plus a content director/developer role, giving her substantive educational content background. She describes a specific and meaningful content accuracy error — assessment-content misalignment — which is exactly the kind of curriculum-level error the rubric identifies as qualifying. She meets the two-part test: she could contribute to content accuracy review immediately, and she describes a specific content accuracy error she personally caught. Score 3 rather than 4 because while she meets the bar clearly, the content director role is mentioned briefly without elaboration on the scope of formal QA ownership, and the transcript does not fully establish she owned a formal primary-reviewer QA function.

quotes: the very first thing we have to do is look at it in its entirety, making sure that the objectives align with the standards
I noticed that the assessments didn't, they didn't align with what we were actually teaching
we have a tendency to look for spelling and accuracy errors
domain_judgment.rationale: Leslie names multiple error categories: standards alignment, assessment-content alignment, spelling, grammar, and consistency. She goes well beyond typos and demonstrates awareness of curriculum-level concerns by citing the assessment misalignment example. This satisfies the Score 3 criteria — at least two distinct error categories with at least one curriculum-level concern backed by a personal example. She does not elaborate on severity ranking or name additional nuanced categories (e.g., accessibility, grade-level appropriateness, factual inaccuracy) that would push to a 4, and the concrete example, while strong, is the only one she develops in depth.

quotes: the very first thing we have to do is look at it in its entirety
making sure that the objectives align with the standards
we have checklists, I have a checklist for almost everything that I do, literally a list for everything I do
so that would keep me consistent with making sure that the lessons continue to be the same throughout the whole entire unit
process_and_methodology.rationale: Leslie describes a recognizable multi-stage process: (1) holistic overview of the lesson in its entirety, (2) standards/objectives alignment check, (3) checklist-based review for consistency across the unit. This maps directly to the rubric's Score 3 example of a three-stage process described informally. She does not describe explicit escalation judgment criteria or edge-case handling (e.g., what to do when unsure) that would push to a 4, but the sequence is clear and replicable.

quotes: the very first thing we have to do is look at it in its entirety, making sure that the objectives align with the standards
I noticed that the assessments didn't, they didn't align with what we were actually teaching
if it's something that I can fix on whatever platform it is that I'm using, then I would go ahead and fix it... anything that I couldn't fix on my own... then I would definitely have to do that
communication.rationale: Leslie's answers are followable throughout. The listener can track her through-line on all three questions without re-listening. Filler phrases ('I say we,' 'you name it,' 'so yeah') are present but per rubric instructions do not reduce the score. Her Q3 answer is brief but articulates a clear decision principle — fix what you can yourself, escalate what you cannot or what protocol requires — which the rubric identifies as sufficient for a 3. She does not demonstrate the precision or structure needed for a 4 (e.g., unambiguous bug ticket language), but communication is clear and organized.

quotes: I know I have three questions that I need to answer, so I wanted to make sure that I keep this under five minutes
I think I've answered the three questions. I hope I'm looking forward to hearing back from you. Thank you so much.
instruction_following_and_professionalis.rationale: Leslie explicitly acknowledges all three questions and addresses each one. Her tone is professional and she closes appropriately. She came prepared with a concrete example for Q2 (assessment misalignment). Q3 is thinner than Q1 and Q2, but it is substantively addressed. No questions were skipped. Meets Score 3 expectations fully.

recommendation_reason: Total score 15 meets the threshold (>=14) and Role Fit 3 meets the gating criterion (>=3).
overall_summary: Leslie is a 29-year English teacher with additional experience as a content director and subject matter expert. She clearly meets the core bar for the role: she has substantive educational content background and describes a specific curriculum-level error she personally caught (assessment-content misalignment). Her process is structured and checklist-driven, covering holistic review, standards alignment, and cross-unit consistency. Domain judgment is solid, covering multiple error categories including curriculum-level concerns. Communication is clear and followable despite informal delivery. The main limitations are that her Q3 answer on bug escalation is brief and lacks elaboration on how she would write up a ticket for a developer, and her content director role is mentioned without enough detail to assess its scope. Overall a competent candidate who meets the advance threshold. Confidence is high given the transcript is complete and all three questions are addressed.

limitations: Transcription quality may affect scoring accuracy.
Scores reflect rubric criteria only, not holistic assessment.
